description: Write a short literary horror fragment in English. Keep the prose natural and unsettling, with rising tension and no explanation. Story context/title: New Home. Motifs or anchor words: gavin, doll, wife, realtor, slightly, wasn, know, phone.
text:
As I stepped into the foyer, the realtor's forced smile still lingered in my mind, like the faint scent of fresh paint. Gavin's eyes, however, were already elsewhere. They wandered the room, as if searching for something I couldn't see. My wife, Emma, held my hand, her grip tightening slightly as we took in the open-plan living area.

The doll on the coffee table seemed out of place, its glassy stare following us as we moved. I tried to shake off the feeling of being watched, but the weight of the doll's gaze lingered. The realtor's words echoed in my mind: "It's the perfect home for you...the perfect home."

I glanced at my phone, the screen dimly lit with a text from the realtor: "Welcome to your new home. You'll get used to the creaks." I didn't know why, but I didn't want to look at the house anymore. I wanted to leave. But Emma's hand remained firm, pulling me deeper into the heart of the new home.

description: Write a short literary horror fragment in English. Keep the prose natural and unsettling, with rising tension and no explanation. Story context/title: Im Not the Only Me. Motifs or anchor words: wish, twin, college, madeline, life, high, maybe, kids.
text:
I lay in bed, the creaking of the old wooden frame harmonizing with the hum of the college's distant generators. My eyes stung from the lingering haze of smoke from last night's cigarettes, and my mind reeled with the wish I'd made to be someone else. Anyone else. 

Madeline, my twin, had always been the life of the party. She'd spin tales of our shared past, of summers spent chasing fireflies and laughing until our sides ached. But I'd never been the high-maintenance, drama-prone one; I'd always been the quiet, reserved one. Maybe that was why I felt like I was living a lie. 

I tossed the covers off, my skin prickling with the chill of the night air. Kids on the floor below us were having a party, their laughter and music a distant hum that threatened to consume me. I had to get out of here, away from the echoes of my twin's voice, away from the constant feeling that I was stuck in someone else's life. I threw on my coat and slipped out into the night, the wish growing louder in my mind: be someone else. Anyone else.

text:
How It Began

Julia's sister, Mary, stood at the kitchen sink, her back to the window as the sun slowly dipped below the horizon. The butter knife lay on the counter, its blade glinting in the fading light. Julia's eyes fixed on the blade, her heart pounding in her chest.

Mary's voice was a low, hypnotic whisper, weaving in and out of Julia's thoughts. "Remember that summer, Julia? The one where we used to play hide-and-seek in the attic? You were so scared, but I found you, didn't I?"

Julia's skin crawled as Mary's words dripped with an otherworldly cadence. The kitchen seemed to grow darker, the shadows twisting around her like grasping fingers. She tried to turn away, but her eyes remained fixed on the knife, its sharp edge glinting like a promise.

Mary's mouth curled into a smile, her eyes gleaming with an unblinking intensity. "And do you remember the taste of the butter, Julia? The way it melted on your tongue, like a slow, cold kiss?"

The GPS, a monotone voice, guides me through the winding roads. We went deeper, the trees twisting into gnarled fingers that clawed at the sky.

Enough of the journey, I thought, my eyes heavy with the weight of countless nights spent awake. Good to be almost there. Maybe this trip would be the last. I checked the rearview mirror, the reflection of the driver beside me twisted, insane. The seat creaked beneath him, the covers of the upholstery worn thin.

The road ahead vanished into the void, swallowed by the shadows. I slowed, my heart heavy with foreboding. The darkness seemed to seep through the windows, tendrils curling around the cab's metal frame. I went slower, the silence growing thicker, until I stopped. The engine sputtered, the only sound in the stillness.

And then, the darkness outside went quiet, too.

My girlfriend, Rachel, lingers by the driver's side door, her eyes fixed on the darkness gathering at the trees' bases. We've been driving for hours, leaving the city's smothering din behind, and I can feel the weight of my father's words: "Don't go there, boy." But with each passing mile, the shadows seem to lengthen, twisting around the trunks like grasping fingers.
